steps.title: Fastest path: mark the figure first
steps.content: Mark the given angle relationships on the figure first: equal angles, vertical angles, supplementary angles, parallel-line angles, and triangle sums of $180^\circ$. Most of these questions become a short angle chase once the givens are marked.

steps.title: Set up the angle relationships
steps.content: Two intersecting lines create four angles. Vertical angles are equal, and adjacent angles are supplementary (sum to 180°). If one angle is $(8x - 130)$°, then its vertical angle is also $(8x - 130)$°, and each of the other two angles is $180 - (8x - 130) = (310 - 8x)$°.

steps.title: List all achievable sums of two angles
steps.content:
The only distinct sums possible from any two of the four angles are:

(1) Two equal acute angles: $(8x-130)+(8x-130) = (16x-260)$°
(2) Two equal obtuse angles: $(310-8x)+(310-8x) = (-16x+620)$°
(3) One of each (a supplementary pair): $(8x-130)+(310-8x) = 180$°

steps.title: Identify which choice is NOT achievable
steps.content:
• Choice A — $\left(-16x+260\right)^\circ$: Does not equal any of the three achievable sums. Not achievable. ✓
  • Choice B — $\left(-16x+620\right)^\circ$: Equals the sum of the two obtuse angles. Achievable; eliminated. ✗
  • Choice C — $\left(16x-260\right)^\circ$: Equals the sum of the two acute angles. Achievable; eliminated. ✗
  • Choice D — $180^\circ$: Equals the sum of one acute and one obtuse angle. Achievable; eliminated. ✗
That is the correct answer.
